note_data.title: Coasts
keyPoints: Rice is a kharif crop requiring warm temperatures for growth 20°C to 30°C and monsoon rain for growth/flooding fields 1270-2000mms
Wheat is a rabi crop requiring mild/warm temperature 10 degree – 20 degree for growing and 20 degrees for ripening
Cash crop farming uses large fields, higher capital required for modern irrigation system i.e. perennial canals, tubewells, sprinklers
Subsistence farming utilizes small land, low capital invested because of poverty, old irrigation methods like shaduf, desi variety of seeds used
Barani farming depends entirely on rainfall/rain-fed area with low banks/bunds constructed and often sheep/goats reared as alternative source of income
Natural inundation farming can only be carried out after flooding, farmers have no control of water supply, variable seasonal cropping
Tobacco growing uses human inputs like tractors for ploughing, harvesters for harvesting and spray of pesticides through machines
Wheat requires plain land for easy use of machinery, alluvial/loam/clay soils, 90 days growing season from Oct-Mar
Rice is not grown in barani farming areas because it requires over 1275mm rainfall in growing season, nowhere in Pakistan has this amount
Winter crop growing enables double or multiple crops, provides jobs throughout the year, increases farmers' income

definitions.definition: Crops grown for sale/profit/commercial rather than for consumption by the farmers
Growing for sale and profit purpose
Crops requiring warm temperatures and monsoon rain for growth
Farming that depends entirely on rainfall/rain-fed area

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Say what you can see - use data from maps/diagrams and geographical terms
Give a reasonable idea with a brief explanation

modelAnswer.question: Explain how a spit is formed. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: A spit forms due to longshore drift transporting sediment along the coast. Waves approach the beach at an angle due to the prevailing wind direction. The swash carries sediment up the beach at an angle but the backwash carries it straight back down due to gravity. This zigzag movement transports sediment along the coast. When the coastline changes direction or there is a bay, the sediment continues to be deposited in the sea, forming a long narrow ridge called a spit.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Waves approach at an angle due to prevailing wind
Mark 3: Swash moves at angle, backwash straight back
Mark 4: Deposition occurs where coastline changes direction

commonMistakes: Confusing kharif and rabi crop seasons and their temperature requirements
Not understanding that rice cannot be grown in barani areas due to insufficient rainfall
Mixing up the human inputs differences between cash crop and subsistence farming
examinerTips: When describing farming types, specify the exact temperature ranges and rainfall requirements
Include specific examples of crops and their growing regions when asked
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cross-section of a cliff showing wave erosion at the base, with labels for high tide, low tide, wave-cut notch, and overhang. Also diagram showing longshore drift with arrows indicating wave direction, swash and backwash movement.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to describe changes in waves as they move towards shore, including wavelength, height and breaking
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q4 (6 marks) — candidates had to assess the relative importance of factors influencing wave generation and characteristics
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q2b (3 marks) — students needed to suggest safety precautions for coastal fieldwork including dangers from waves, tides and cliffs
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — required explanation of coastal erosion processes and their relationship to wave energy

workedExample.question: Describe the changes in waves shown in Fig. 4.1 as they move towards the shore. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify what happens to wavelength - measure distance between wave crests in deep vs shallow water
Step 2: Observe wave height changes - compare wave amplitude in deep water to near shore
Step 3: Note the breaking process - explain when and why waves become unstable
Step 4: Link changes to water depth - explain the cause of these transformations
workedExample.answer: As waves move towards shore, the wavelength decreases due to friction with the seabed in shallow water. Wave height increases as energy becomes compressed into smaller space. Waves become steeper and eventually break when water depth is approximately 1.3 times the wave height. The wave crest moves faster than the base, causing the wave to topple forward.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: wavelength decreases, wave height increases, waves break/become steeper, reference to shallow water/seabed friction

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused wave refraction with wave breaking' — always distinguish between waves bending around headlands versus waves collapsing in shallow water
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often failed to link wave changes to water depth' — always explain that friction with seabed in shallow water causes wave transformation

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently omitted safety precautions in fieldwork questions' — always mention checking tide times, staying away from cliff edges, and working from safe positions when discussing coastal studies
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a cross-section showing wave transformation from deep to shallow water. Include: deep water with long wavelength/low height waves on left, gradually decreasing wavelength and increasing height toward shore, seabed rising toward beach, and final breaking wave with crest toppling forward. Label wavelength, wave height, water depth, and direction of wave movement. Add annotations explaining friction with seabed causes changes.
note_data.quickRecap: Pakistan agriculture involves different farming systems including cash crop farming with modern inputs, subsistence farming with traditional methods, and barani rain-fed farming. Crops are classified as kharif (warm season like rice) or rabi (cool season like wheat) based on temperature and rainfall requirements. Different regions specialize in different crops based on natural conditions and farming methods available.
